summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Rock Cycle
summary_sections.content_html:
The rock cycle describes how rocks continuously transform from one type to another through geological processes:

  • Igneous → Sedimentary: weathering and erosion break igneous rock into sediments, which are deposited, compacted, and cemented.
  • Sedimentary → Metamorphic: burial increases heat and pressure, transforming sedimentary rock.
  • Metamorphic → Igneous: further heat causes melting to magma, which cools into igneous rock.
  • Any rock type can become any other type—the cycle has no fixed starting point.
  • Uplift exposes buried rocks to weathering; subduction pulls surface rocks deep into the mantle.

The rock cycle is driven by Earth's internal heat (plate tectonics, volcanism) and external energy from the Sun (weathering, erosion).
